examples.title: Algorithm: Binary Search Tree Operations
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating core BST operations: search, insert, delete, and in-order traversal
examples.code:
// BST Node Definition
STRUCTURE Node
 DATA value
 POINTER left
 POINTER right
END STRUCTURE

// Create a new node
ALGORITHM createNode(value)
 INPUT: value - the data to store
 OUTPUT: pointer to new node

BEGIN
 newNode ← allocate memory for Node
 IF newNode = NULL THEN
 RETURN NULL // Memory allocation failed
 END IF
 newNode.value ← value
 newNode.left ← NULL
 newNode.right ← NULL
 RETURN newNode
END

// Insert a value into BST
ALGORITHM insert(root, value)
 INPUT: root - root of BST, value - value to insert
 OUTPUT: root of modified BST

BEGIN
 IF root = NULL THEN
 RETURN createNode(value)
 END IF

 IF value < root.value THEN
 root.left ← insert(root.left, value)
 ELSE IF value > root.value THEN
 root.right ← insert(root.right, value)
 END IF
 // If value equals root.value, do nothing (no duplicates)

 RETURN root
END

// Search for a value in BST
ALGORITHM search(root, value)
 INPUT: root - root of BST, value - value to search
 OUTPUT: pointer to node if found, NULL otherwise

BEGIN
 IF root = NULL OR root.value = value THEN
 RETURN root
 END IF

 IF value < root.value THEN
 RETURN search(root.left, value)
 ELSE
 RETURN search(root.right, value)
 END IF
END

// Find minimum value node
ALGORITHM findMin(node)
 INPUT: node - starting node
 OUTPUT: pointer to node with minimum value

BEGIN
 current ← node
 WHILE current.left ≠ NULL DO
 current ← current.left
 END WHILE
 RETURN current
END

// Delete a value from BST
ALGORITHM delete(root, value)
 INPUT: root - root of BST, value - value to delete
 OUTPUT: root of modified BST

BEGIN
 IF root = NULL THEN
 RETURN NULL
 END IF

 IF value < root.value THEN
 root.left ← delete(root.left, value)
 ELSE IF value > root.value THEN
 root.right ← delete(root.right, value)
 ELSE
 // Found node to delete - handle 3 cases
 // Case 1: Leaf node (no children)
 IF root.left = NULL AND root.right = NULL THEN
 free(root)
 RETURN NULL
 // Case 2: One child
 ELSE IF root.left = NULL THEN
 temp ← root.right
 free(root)
 RETURN temp
 ELSE IF root.right = NULL THEN
 temp ← root.left
 free(root)
 RETURN temp
 // Case 3: Two children
 ELSE
 temp ← findMin(root.right) // Find in-order successor
 root.value ← temp.value
 root.right ← delete(root.right, temp.value)
 END IF
 END IF

 RETURN root
END

// In-order traversal (gives sorted order)
ALGORITHM inorder(root)
 INPUT: root - root of BST
 OUTPUT: prints all values in sorted order

BEGIN
 IF root ≠ NULL THEN
 inorder(root.left)
 PRINT root.value
 inorder(root.right)
 END IF
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the fundamental operations on a Binary Search Tree. The key property is that left subtree values are less than the node, and right subtree values are greater. The insert and search operations have O(h) time complexity where h is height. In a balanced BST, this is O(log n), but in worst case (skewed tree) it becomes O(n). The delete operation handles three cases: leaf node removal, single child replacement, and two children where we replace with in-order successor.

examples.title: Python: Binary Search Tree Implementation
examples.description: Complete Python implementation of BST with insert, search, delete, and traversal operations
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates a complete BST with insert, search, delete, and traversal operations. The class-based approach encapsulates all tree operations. The delete method handles all three cases: leaf node removal, single child replacement, and two children where we use the in-order successor. The in-order traversal returns values in sorted order. Time complexity for balanced BST is O(log n) for all operations, but degrades to O(n) in worst case.
examples.timeComplexity: O(log n) average, O(n) worst case
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storage, O(h) for recursion stack where h is height

examples.title: C: Binary Search Tree Implementation
examples.description: Complete C implementation of BST with insert, search, delete, and traversal operations
examples.explanation: This C implementation provides a complete BST with all fundamental operations. The struct-based Node definition allows dynamic memory allocation. Key features include: recursive insert and search (O(log n) average), delete with three cases handled, and three traversal methods (in-order gives sorted output). The implementation uses malloc/free for memory management. The findMin function locates the in-order successor for the two-children deletion case. All operations have O(h) time complexity where h is tree height.
examples.timeComplexity: O(log n) average, O(n) worst case
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storage, O(h) for recursion stack
